      Insider trading
   =20
 =20

Look, if your spouse works as a mergers and acquisitions manager at a big p=
ublic company, and her company acquires another public company,  and you =
=E2=80=9Cmethodically sold all [your] positions in both [your] individual b=
rokerage account and [your] Roth IRA (totaling approximately $2.16 million)=
=E2=80=9D to buy shares of the target stock before the deal was announced, =
and then=C2=A0you sell the target stock when the deal is announced and make=
 a profit of $1.76 million: That is insider trading and you will get arrest=
ed. I don=E2=80=99t need to know any more facts. Your spouse worked on the =
deal, you sold everything you owned to buy the target stock, the regulators=
 can very=C2=A0easily put those two facts together, and when they ask you w=
hat happened and you are like =E2=80=9Ccrazy coincidence right?=E2=80=9D th=
ey will absolutely not believe you, nor should they. Bad, bad, bad look.
No, the only question is: Will your=C2=A0spouse=C2=A0get arrested? Because =
there are   two possibilities here:
=09Your spouse used the inside information from her job to tell you about t=
he coming deal, knowing that you would trade on it. She =E2=80=9Ctipped=E2=
=80=9D you with the inside information,=C2=A0expecting (as your spouse) to =
share in your profits. So she, like you, is guilty of insider trading.=09Or=

cret from you, but you snuck onto her computer at night and read her email =
and figured it out. Or, much more likely, she mentioned to you what she was=
 working on, but she expected (as your spouse) that you would keep her conf=
idences and not go out and trade on the information like a moron. In that c=
ase,=C2=A0you=C2=A0are guilty of insider trading =E2=80=94=C2=A0you misappr=
opriated information that you got from her in breach of a =E2=80=9Cduty of =
trust or confidence=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0but=C2=A0she=C2=A0is just an in=
nocent victim.

In general this seems like a hard question for investigators to resolve. (I=

mation, the=C2=A0least=C2=A0you can do is say that it was all your idea and=
 she knew nothing about it, whether or not that is true.) Sometimes it=E2=

I   wrote a few weeks ago about Bill Ackman=E2=80=99s plan to raise a new c=
losed-end fund for US retail investors. I started by talking about Ackman=
=E2=80=99s existing closed-end fund for retail investors, Pershing Square H=
oldings (PSH), which is listed in Europe and which, at this point, is Ackma=
n=E2=80=99s main investing vehicle,  holding about $12.3 billion of his $15=
.8=C2=A0billion of net assets under management.
  [1]
=C2=A0PSH is mostly=C2=A0pretty simple: It does some hedging and derivative=
s trading, but mostly=C2=A0it owns the stocks of a small number (currently =
10) of large public companies. It has a small amount of debt. And it charge=
s hedge-fund-ish fees, an annual management fee of 1.5% of assets and a per=
formance fee of 16% of gains.
So you can pretty easily figure out PSH=E2=80=99s net asset value (NAV):=C2=
=A0Add up the value of its liquid public holdings, subtract the debt, and y=
ou get a decent estimate of how much each share of PSH is worth. And in fac=
t PSH=E2=80=99s=C2=A0net asset value is reported regularly=C2=A0on Bloomber=
g and on  PSH=E2=80=99s website. But PSH=E2=80=99s shares, which trade on t=
he Amsterdam and London stock exchanges (and over the counter in the US
  [2]
), generally trade at a discount to NAV. On Friday, the stock closed at $48=
.80, a 26.3% discount to the $66.21 NAV. I wrote:=C2=A0=E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=
=99t know exactly why PSH trades at a discount, but,=E2=80=9D and then went=
 on to talk about other things.
Several readers emailed me to propose explanations for why PSH trades at a =
discount, but the=C2=A0 simplest explanation is that the discount capitaliz=
es the fees.
  [3]
=C2=A0Say you have $100 to invest. PSH   consists mostly=C2=A0of a short=C2=
=A0public=C2=A0list of publicly traded stocks.
  [4]
=C2=A0You can look at the list of stocks that PSH owns and then replicate i=
t yourself, just buying $100 of those stocks. Then you will own $100 worth =
of stocks. If the stocks go up 20% in a year, then next year you will own $=
120 worth of stocks.=C2=A0
Or=C2=A0you can put $100 into PSH. Assume counterfactually that PSH trades =
at no discount: You put in $100 for $100 worth of NAV. Then you will (indir=
ectly) own $100 worth of those same stocks. If the stocks go up 20% in a ye=
ar, then next year you will own =E2=80=94=C2=A0well, you=E2=80=99ll pay $1.=
50 in management fees and $3.20 (16% of 20% of $100) of performance fees, s=
o you=E2=80=99ll own $115.30 worth of stocks. This is worse. The following =
year you will also pay fees, and on plausible assumptions those fees will e=
nd up eating a large chunk of your gains, enough to explain a 26% discount.
  [5]

Again, just buying the stocks that PSH holds should cost you $100. PSH is a=
 package consisting of (1) the stocks it holds (2)=C2=A0minus=C2=A0its fees=
, so it=C2=A0should=C2=A0cost less than $100. Thus the discount.
Now, this argument is far too crude. The counterargument is that PSH is=C2=
=A0actually=C2=A0a package consisting of (1) stocks (2) minus fees (3)=C2=
=A0plus=C2=A0Bill Ackman=E2=80=99s investing skill, which allows him to cha=
nge=C2=A0the portfolio over time, selling=C2=A0the stocks that will go down=
 and instead buying other, better=C2=A0stocks that will go up. If you just =
buy his most recently disclosed portfolio, you avoid the fees but also miss=
 out on his investing skill. Buying the stocks Ackman owns now is not actua=
lly a good substitute for buying the stocks=C2=A0(and derivatives, etc.) th=
at Ackman will own over time.
But the market, at least for PSH,=C2=A0seems to attribute more (negative) v=
alue to the fees than to the skill. And part of what we talked about last t=
ime was that Ackman=E2=80=99s new US fund seems to be a bet that US retail =
investors will attribute more value to the skill. Also the US fees will be =
lower.
In any case, this situation does suggest=C2=A0a simple trade, which is =E2=
=80=9Cstop paying the fees and give us the stocks=E2=80=9D:
=09You can buy PSH=E2=80=99s shares=C2=A0at a 26% discount to net asset val=
ue. PSH is a pot full of $12.2 billion worth of stock, but that pot trades =
for=C2=A0$9 billion.=09So buy=C2=A0all=C2=A0of PSH, or at least a controlli=
ng stake.=09Crack PSH open, take out the $12.2 billion worth of stocks, giv=
e them to yourself and stop paying the fees.=09Sell them if you want, whate=
ver; the point is that you bought $12.2 billion of stocks for $9 billion.

You can=E2=80=99t really do that, for various reasons. One reason is that A=
ckman himself owns a lot of the shares.=C2=A0Another is that if you actuall=
y go and buy all the PSH shares you=E2=80=99ll push up the price and the ma=
th might no longer work.
  [6]
 Still the fact that a pot of $12.2 billion of large publicly traded stocks=
 is available for $9 billion does create a temptation.
For instance, Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Yiqin Shen   reported last week:

Elliott Investment Management targeted Pershing Square Holdings in 2017, wh=

 Ackman to liquidate his listed company.

=E2=80=9CI envisioned an end where the permanent capital vehicle ends up ge=

ucceed.=E2=80=9D

Here are the  interview=C2=A0and Ackman=E2=80=99s X post. Closed-end fund a=
ctivism  is a popular trade, and Ackman=E2=80=99s   buddy Boaz Weinstein ha=
s   made a crusade of it.=C2=A0(Though not against Ackman; that would be ru=
de.) In some ways it is the most schematic possible form of   activist inve=
sting:
=09There is a company with publicly traded stock.=C2=A0=09The stock price i=
s below the company=E2=80=99s fundamental value.=09The company is paying it=
s management a lot.=09An activist buys up all the stock and tries to kick o=
ut management.=09Management says =E2=80=9Cno, you don=E2=80=99t understand,=
 we are getting paid for creating long-term value, and the market is too fo=

Sometimes management=E2=80=99s long-term plans are good and worth the money=

When we previously talked about PSH, I emphasized what a good idea it was, =
and is, for Ackman (or any other hedge fund manager)=C2=A0to have a permane=
nt capital vehicle. If you run a hedge fund and you have disappointing perf=

have to give it to them, and then you don=E2=80=99t run a hedge fund anymor=
e. If you run a closed-end fund and you have disappointing performance, no =
one can ask for their money back =E2=80=94=C2=A0that=E2=80=99s kind of what=
 =E2=80=9Cclosed-end=E2=80=9D means =E2=80=94=C2=A0and so you get to keep r=
unning a hedge fund forever. But not literally; you can=E2=80=99t keep runn=

      Private credit
   =20
 =20

Just a very dumb simple model of private credit is:
=09People =E2=80=94=C2=A0asset managers,  sovereign wealth funds,  banks,  =
rich individuals, whoever =E2=80=94=C2=A0have flocked into private credit=
=C2=A0because it=C2=A0can  offer higher yields than traditional bonds and s=
yndicated bank loans.=09Why would you want to=C2=A0borrow=C2=A0from them? =
=E2=80=9CPrivate credit has higher yields than bank loans=E2=80=9D is a gre=
at pitch to=C2=A0lenders, who want to receive higher yields, but it is a ba=
d pitch to=C2=A0borrowers, who want to pay lower yields.

There are answers. The one that I have probably heard the most is that priv=
ate-credit lenders can be faster and more decisive: If you are a company lo=
oking for a loan, or a private equity firm looking for buyout financing, go=
ing to one or two direct lenders and saying =E2=80=9Ccan you lend me the mo=
ney=E2=80=9D and having them reply =E2=80=9Cyes, here are the terms, sign h=
ere=E2=80=9D is better than going to a bank and having them say =E2=80=9Cwe=
=E2=80=99ll need a one-month marketing process, here are the indicative ter=
ms but we reserve the right to flex based on market conditions.=E2=80=9D In=
 private credit, borrowers deal directly with the people making the lending=
 decision; in bonds and bank loans, they deal with intermediaries. In a cho=
ppy market, it is worth paying up a bit for certainty of execution.
There are a few other answers. If you don=E2=80=99t deal with intermediarie=
s (banks), you don=E2=80=99t pay their fees; the economic model of private =
credit might be not just =E2=80=9Cprivate credit lenders charge more than s=
yndicated loan and bond investors=E2=80=9D but=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cprivate credit=
 lenders charge more but you save on bank=C2=A0underwriting fees.=E2=80=9D =
Also you could imagine private credit firms being nicer to deal with if you=
 run into trouble and need to restructure your debts: You have fewer lender=
s, you know who they are, they can=E2=80=99t sell their loans, they probabl=
y all work on the same floor=C2=A0as private equity investors=C2=A0so they =
sympathize with your troubled private equity buyout, etc.
Still my general view of lending markets is that if one product offers some=
 long appealing list of qualitative advantages, and the other product is th=
ree basis points cheaper, everyone=C2=A0prefers the cheaper product. The Fi=
nancial Times reports:

Roughly $10bn of so-called private credit loans have been refinanced in pub=
lic markets, as borrowers pay down burdensome loans in favour of a cheaper =
alternative, according to data from Bank of America.
Private equity firms that buy out companies are taking advantage of a recov=
ery in global corporate bond and loan markets, after the Federal Reserve si=
gnalled that inflation had been sufficiently tamed for it to begin cutting =
interest rates.
That shift has opened the door for investment banks to pitch hard for busin=
ess that they lost to private lenders after rates dramatically rose in 2022=
, with the banks hoping for a revival of lucrative fees.
=E2=80=9CPressure is coming from sponsors for the coupons to be cut and thi=
s is just a race to the bottom between the banks and the direct lenders,=E2=
=80=9D said Neha Khoda, a strategist at Bank of America. =E2=80=A6
The changing market conditions offer an opportunity to banks, which were sa=
ddled with billions of dollars of losses on the deals they agreed to financ=
e in 2022. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The losses limited lenders=E2=80=99 interest in e=
xtending new loans, and when they did the terms were often too expensive fo=
r private equity firms to turn to.

This is the good and bad side of the =E2=80=9Cprivate credit is faster and =
more decisive=E2=80=9D point: If you got a commitment letter from some bank=
s for a leveraged buyout financing in 2022, and then rates shot up, then wh=
en that financing closed months later you got an outrageously good deal. (A=
sk  Elon Musk.) But the banks were burned by the outrageously good deals th=
ey gave out in 2022, so they could only offer pretty bad deals in 2023. Now=
 they are back.

If you do a =E2=80=9Cprivate IPO,=E2=80=9D what does the P stand for? =E2=
=80=9CPrivate initial public offering=E2=80=9D? You see the problem. Here=
=E2=80=99s  the Wall Street Journal:

The concept is being bandied about on Wall Street as investors and bankers =

ors such as mutual funds or sovereign-wealth funds, sidestepping the tradit=
ional IPO process.=C2=A0
Private IPOs don=E2=80=99t come with the splashy bell-ringing ceremony of a=
 traditional debut or result in publicly traded stock. They do allow compan=
ies to avoid the potential embarrassment of a new listing falling flat.=C2=
=A0
Some on Wall Street balk at the name, seeing it as window dressing for priv=
ate placements, which are sales of shares from one private owner to another=

till, are playing with semantics to drum up business.
What=E2=80=99s more, some mutual-fund managers who have been approached to =
do private IPOs are wary. While they might get better prices and bigger all=

=80=94is a big drawback. If the managers buy stakes this way, it isn=E2=80=
=99t clear when they would be able to sell them.

We have   talked about this phenomenon before, and I have argued that it ki=

arkets to put a higher valuation on reasonably mature companies than the pr=

that you can=E2=80=99t sell. If you are a company considering going public,=
 and you decide not to because you don=E2=80=99t think you=E2=80=99ll get a=
 good enough price, why would you get a better price in a =E2=80=9Cprivate =

I suggested a few weak counterarguments:
=09Maybe private investors are systematically mistaken about valuation? See=
ms weird.=09Maybe    illiquidity=C2=A0is actually valuable: If you can=E2=
=80=99t sell a stock, you won=E2=80=99t sell it in a foolish panic, so mayb=
e your long-term returns will be higher. Again it would be strange if this =
were=C2=A0generally=C2=A0true.=C2=A0=09Maybe going public actually destroys=
 value, because of=C2=A0public financial reporting or compliance costs or s=

Eh. Anyway the =E2=80=9Csemantics=E2=80=9D point seems right. Lots of priva=
te companies have done trades =E2=80=94=C2=A0private placements, Series G f=
undraising rounds,   employee tender offers, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0that have =
the essential shape of =E2=80=9Cthe company stays private but somebody buys=
 stock.=E2=80=9D What makes a trade a =E2=80=9Cprivate IPO=E2=80=9D instead=
 of =E2=80=9Cselling stock=E2=80=9D? Is it that the buyer of the stock is a=
 mutual fund?=C2=A0I think the answer might be =E2=80=9Cit is pitched to yo=
u that way by an investment bank.=E2=80=9D

Last week I   described BOXX, the Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF, an exc=
hange-traded fund that is meant to pay money-market interest rates without =
taxes, using options strategies. I wrote: =E2=80=9CThe first thing you want=
 to do is turn interest income into capital gains. =E2=80=A6 In some sense =
this should be easy.=E2=80=9D Some assets pay you a return that is called =
=E2=80=9Cinterest,=E2=80=9D which is fixed and paid in installments and tax=
ed at ordinary income rates; other assets pay you a return that is called =
=E2=80=9Cappreciation,=E2=80=9D which you receive when you sell the asset a=
nd which is taxed at lower capital gains rates. There is some economic equi=
valence between these things; assets that don=E2=80=99t pay interest genera=
lly appreciate at some rate that compensates you for the use=C2=A0of your m=
oney, sort of like interest does.
The trick is to find an asset that appreciates at the risk-free rate, i.e.,=
 that reliably pays you roughly the same return as short-term Treasury bill=
s, but in the form of =E2=80=9Cappreciation=E2=80=9D rather than =E2=80=9Ci=
nterest.=E2=80=9D The simplest way to do that would be with a zero-coupon b=
ond =E2=80=94=C2=A0a bond that doesn=E2=80=99t make quarterly interest paym=
ents, but just pays you back more at the end than you put in at the beginni=
ng =E2=80=94=C2=A0but that=E2=80=99s=C2=A0too=C2=A0simple, and the US tax c=
ode looks through that trick and treats the appreciation on a zero-coupon b=
ond as interest.=C2=A0
So the second-simplest way to do it=C2=A0is by buying a=C2=A0stock=C2=A0(a =
classic capital asset) and=C2=A0hedging out the stock-price risk. For insta=
nce, if you buy a stock and simultaneously sell it in a fixed-price forward=
 contract, you have effectively hedged out the stock-price risk. And that=
=E2=80=99s roughly what BOXX does, in the form of =E2=80=9Cbox spreads=E2=
=80=9D on S&P 500 index options (get synthetically long the index by buying=
 a call option and selling a put, get synthetically short the index by sell=
ing a call and buying a put, and do those things at different strike prices=
 so that you lock in some payoff in the future).=C2=A0
But several readers emailed to point out that, at least sometimes,=C2=A0the=
 US tax code looks through=C2=A0that=C2=A0trick too.  Section 1258 of the I=
nternal Revenue Code recharacterizes some capital gains as ordinary income,=
 if those gains are=C2=A0effectively=C2=A0interest on a =E2=80=9Cconversion=
 transaction,=E2=80=9D meaning a transaction where you (1) buy an asset and=
 (2) simultaneously enter into a contract to sell the asset at a fixed pric=
e.
  [7]
=C2=A0So a simple transaction like =E2=80=9Cbuy a stock and sell it forward=
=E2=80=9D probably produces ordinary income, not capital gains.
Do BOXX=E2=80=99s box spreads produce ordinary income? My understanding is =
that there is a=C2=A0common view=C2=A0that box spreads are=C2=A0not =E2=80=
=9Cconversion transactions,=E2=80=9D and in particular that box spreads wit=
h=C2=A0index options are covered by different rules. But I do not claim to =
be an expert, or to give tax advice, and apparently some people=C2=A0disagr=
ee.=C2=A0

ng to do a rights offering, hoping to raise $200 million at a =E2=80=9Cpost=
-money valuation =E2=80=A6 of $220 million to $225 million, a 99% drop from=
 the $22 billion valuation that the startup had attained in 2022,=E2=80=9D =
oops oops oops oops oops. Raising $200 million at a $220=C2=A0million post-=
money valuation
  [8]
=C2=A0is =E2=80=9Chey, turns out we blew all your money and the company is =
worthless, want to try again?=E2=80=9D Why would they want to try again? An=
yway  here=E2=80=99s Byju:

Byju Raveendran, the founder of eponymous edtech group Byju=E2=80=99s, told=
 employees on Saturday that he continues to remain the chief executive of t=
he startup and that rumors of his firing have been =E2=80=9Cgreatly exagger=
ated,=E2=80=9D a day after a shareholder group voted to remove him at an em=
ergency general meeting.
In a 758-word letter, content of which was reviewed by TechCrunch, Raveendr=
an claimed that the shareholders violated several =E2=80=9Cessential=E2=80=
=9D local rules.
The shareholder group, which included Prosus Ventures and Peak XV Ventures,=
 said in a statement on Friday that the investors =E2=80=9Cunanimously pass=
ed=E2=80=9D the resolutions that seek to enact, among other things, address=
ing governance, compliance issues, financial mismanagement, reconstitution =
of its board and a change in leadership =E2=80=9Cso that it is no longer co=
ntrolled by the founders of T&L.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6
Raveendran claimed in the letter that the extraordinary general meeting lac=
ked the minimum quorum and failed to win majority support for proposed reso=
lutions. Raveendran claimed the EGM was convened without adhering to the pr=
ocedures set out by law and only 35 of Byju=E2=80=99s 170 total shareholder=
s attended, representing around 45% ownership in the company. =E2=80=A6
=E2=80=9COur rights issue has seen an overwhelming response. In fact, such =
has been the scale of its success that even those who were sitting on the f=
ence are now rushing to get a piece of the action. This momentum is irrever=
sible, and our comeback is now inevitable,=E2=80=9D Raveendran told employe=
es.

Ah. If I ran a company that was once worth $22 billion and is now worth $25=
 million, I would be hesitant to say things like =E2=80=9Cthis momentum is =
irreversible,=E2=80=9D but I guess that=E2=80=99s why I don=E2=80=99t.

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Look, if your spouse works as a mergers and ac=
quisitions manager at a big public company, and her company acquires anothe=
r public company, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9maWxlcy9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2NvbXBsYWl=
udHMvMjAyNC9jb21wLXByMjAyNC0yNC5wZGY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe6c72a9a" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;"> and you</a> =E2=80=9Cmethodically sold all [your] positions in both [=
your] individual brokerage account and [your] Roth IRA (totaling approximat=
ely $2.16 million)=E2=80=9D to buy shares of the target stock before the de=
al was announced, and then=C2=A0you sell the target stock when the deal is =
announced and make a profit of $1.76 million: That is insider trading and y=
ou will get arrested. I don=E2=80=99t need to know any more facts. Your spo=
use worked on the deal, you sold everything you owned to buy the target sto=
ck, the regulators can very=C2=A0easily put those two facts together, and w=
hen they ask you what happened and you are like =E2=80=9Ccrazy coincidence =
right?=E2=80=9D they will absolutely not believe you, nor should they. Bad,=
 bad, bad look.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No, the only question is: Will your=C2=A0<em>s=
pouse</em><em>=C2=A0</em>get arrested? Because there are <a href=3D"https:/=
/link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxv=
b21iZXJnLmNvbS92aWV3L2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTQtMDgtMTgvb25lLWdvbGYtY291cnNlLXRoYXQ=
tcy1ncmVhdC1mb3Itc3RvY2stdGlwcz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMjI2MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbW=
VkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDAyMjYmdXRtX2Nhb=
XBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB491b33bd" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  two pos=
sibilities here</a>:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Your spouse used the inside informati=
on from her job to tell you about the coming deal, knowing that you would t=
rade on it. She =E2=80=9Ctipped=E2=80=9D you with the inside information,=
=C2=A0expecting (as your spouse) to share in your profits. So she, like you=
, is guilty of insider trading.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Or=
 your spouse=C2=A0<em>didn=E2=80=99t </em>tip you. Maybe she kept the infor=
mation secret from you, but you snuck onto her computer at night and read h=
er email and figured it out. Or, much more likely, she mentioned to you wha=
t she was working on, but she expected (as your spouse) that you would keep=
 her confidences and not go out and trade on the information like a moron. =
In that case,=C2=A0<em>you=C2=A0</em>are guilty of insider trading =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0you misappropriated information that you got from her in breach of=
 a =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/344788=
02.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L2Nmci90ZXh0LzE3LzI0MC4xMGI1L=
TI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB40ef735c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"i=
temscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none =
!important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-=
bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">duty of trust or confidence</=
a>=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0but=C2=A0<em>she=C2=A0</em>is just an innocent v=
ictim.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In general this seems like a hard question for=
 investigators to resolve. (If you=C2=A0<em>have=C2=A0</em>gone and insider=
 traded on your spouse=E2=80=99s information, the=C2=A0<em>least=C2=A0</em>=
you can do is say that it was all your idea and she knew nothing about it, =
whether or not that is true.) Sometimes it=E2=80=99s easier though. Here=E2=
=80=99s a pretty grim <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cl=
ick/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjA=
yNC0yNA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB080e0faa" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> US Securities and Excha=
nge Commission case</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Securities and Exchange Commission [Thursd=
ay] charged Tyler Loudon of Houston, Texas, with insider trading ahead of a=
 February 2023 announcement that London-based oil and gas company BP p.l.c.=
 agreed to acquire TravelCenters of America Inc., a full-service truck stop=
 and travel center company headquartered in Ohio. Loudon allegedly made $1.=
76 million in illegal profits from his trading.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">According to the SEC=E2=80=99s complaint, Loud=
on allegedly misappropriated material, nonpublic information about the prop=
osed acquisition from his wife, a BP mergers and acquisitions manager who w=
orked on the planned deal. The SEC alleges that Loudon overheard several of=
 his wife=E2=80=99s work-related conversations about the merger while she w=
as working remotely.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Loudon also <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVzdGljZS5nb3YvdXNh=
by1zZHR4L3ByL2luc2lkZXItdHJhZGluZy1odXNiYW5kLWlsbGVnYWxseS1wcm9maXRzLTE3bS1=
hZnRlci11c2luZy13aWZlcy1wcml2YXRlLWNvbXBhbnk/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6f3c6=
b4f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> pleaded guilty</a> to criminal charges. The <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2V=
jLmdvdi9maWxlcy9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2NvbXBsYWludHMvMjAyNC9jb21wLXByMjAyNC0yNC5wZG=
Y/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cCe6c72a9a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"it=
emscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !=
important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-b=
ottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> SEC complaint</a> describes h=
ow Loudon found out about the deal, which is standard post-Covid <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDctMDMvaW5zaWRlci10cmF=
kaW5nLWlzLWJldHRlci1mcm9tLWhvbWU_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDIyNjI0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX2=
1lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjQwMjI2JnV0bV9jY=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Loudon and=C2=A0his wife generally=
 worked in home offices within 20 feet of each other. As a result, they fre=
quently overheard and witnessed each other=E2=80=99s work-related conversat=
ions and video conferences. In late December 2022, Loudon and his wife trav=
eled to Rome, staying in a small Airbnb where Loudon=E2=80=99s wife regular=
ly worked on the TA acquisition and discussed the deal while Loudon was sea=
ted nearby.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But also: =E2=80=9CLoudon=E2=80=99s wife ackno=
wledged discussing aspects of the acquisition with Loudon during the normal=
 course of marital communications.=E2=80=9D This is not a case where she ri=
gorously kept the deal secret from him, and he broke into her laptop: It=E2=
=80=99s a case where a husband and wife talked about their days, and she tr=
usted that he would not go and insider trade on the deal. Because why would=
 he do that? That would be crazy!</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway, after the deal, regulators=C2=A0<em>of=
 course=C2=A0</em>checked into whether anyone who worked on the deal, or th=
eir contacts, traded in the stock. Loudon=E2=80=99s wife mentioned this to =
him, and he realized what he had done:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One week later, on April 3, 2023, =
Loudon confessed to his wife that he had traded in TA prior to the acquisit=
ion announcement. Loudon did not tell her the number of shares he purchased=
 or the profits he realized from their sale. Loudon told his wife that he h=
ad bought the TA shares because he wanted to make enough money so that she =
did not have to work long hours anymore.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CI was doing it for you!=E2=80=9D Bad!=
</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Stunned by this revelation, Loudon=
=E2=80=99s wife reported the trading to her supervisor at BP. In turn, BP p=
laced her on administrative leave. BP reviewed Loudon=E2=80=99s wife=E2=80=
=99s emails and texts, finding no evidence that she knowingly leaked the ac=
quisition to Loudon or otherwise knew of her husband=E2=80=99s trading. BP =
nonetheless terminated her employment.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Well, so, she=E2=80=99s not working long hours=
 anymore.</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">After Loudon=E2=80=99s confession,=
 Loudon=E2=80=99s wife moved out of their house and generally ceased all co=
ntact with Loudon. A few weeks later, Loudon delivered a handwritten note t=
o his wife apologizing for violating her trust and asking for her forgivene=
ss. Loudon=E2=80=99s wife initiated divorce proceedings in June 2023.</p></=
blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think that=E2=80=99s the worst insider tradi=
ng case I=E2=80=99ve ever read? Between her prompt reporting to BP, their r=
eview of her texts and emails, and the <em>immediate divorce</em>, it seems=
 pretty clear she wasn=E2=80=99t in on the insider trading. Still, terrible=
.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are answers. The one that I have probabl=
y heard the most is that private-credit lenders can be faster and more deci=
sive: If you are a company looking for a loan, or a private equity firm loo=
king for buyout financing, going to one or two direct lenders and saying =
=E2=80=9Ccan you lend me the money=E2=80=9D and having them reply =E2=80=9C=
yes, here are the terms, sign here=E2=80=9D is better than going to a bank =
and having them say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ll need a one-month marketing proce=
ss, here are the indicative terms but we reserve the right to flex based on=
 market conditions.=E2=80=9D In private credit, borrowers deal directly wit=
h the people making the lending decision; in bonds and bank loans, they dea=
l with intermediaries. In a choppy market, it is worth paying up a bit for =
certainty of execution.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are a few other answers. If you don=E2=
=80=99t deal with intermediaries (banks), you don=E2=80=99t pay their fees;=
 the economic model of private credit might be not just =E2=80=9Cprivate cr=
edit lenders charge more than syndicated loan and bond investors=E2=80=9D b=
ut=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cprivate credit lenders charge more but you save on bank=C2=
=A0underwriting fees.=E2=80=9D Also you could imagine private credit firms =
being nicer to deal with if you run into trouble and need to restructure yo=
ur debts: You have fewer lenders, you know who they are, they can=E2=80=99t=
 sell their loans, they probably all work on the same floor=C2=A0as private=
 equity investors=C2=A0so they sympathize with your troubled private equity=
 buyout, etc.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still my general view of lending markets is th=
at if one product offers some long appealing list of qualitative advantages=
, and the other product is three basis points cheaper, everyone=C2=A0prefer=
s the cheaper product. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=

;">Financial Times reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Roughly $10bn of so-called private credit loan=
s have been refinanced in public markets, as borrowers pay down burdensome =
loans in favour of a cheaper alternative, according to data from Bank of Am=
erica.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Private equity firms that buy out companies ar=
e taking advantage of a recovery in global corporate bond and loan markets,=
 after the Federal Reserve signalled that inflation had been sufficiently t=
amed for it to begin cutting interest rates.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That shift has opened the door for investment =
banks to pitch hard for business that they lost to private lenders after ra=
tes dramatically rose in 2022, with the banks hoping for a revival of lucra=
tive fees.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CPressure is coming from sponsors for =
the coupons to be cut and this is just a race to the bottom between the ban=
ks and the direct lenders,=E2=80=9D said Neha Khoda, a strategist at Bank o=
f America. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The changing market conditions offer an opport=
unity to banks, which were saddled with billions of dollars of losses on th=
e deals they agreed to finance in 2022. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The losses limited l=
enders=E2=80=99 interest in extending new loans, and when they did the term=
s were often too expensive for private equity firms to turn to.</p>
</blockquote>

private credit is faster and more decisive=E2=80=9D point: If you got a com=
mitment letter from some banks for a leveraged buyout financing in 2022, an=
d then rates shot up, then when that financing closed months later you got =
an outrageously good deal. (Ask <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you do a =E2=80=9Cprivate IPO,=E2=80=9D wha=

You see the problem. Here=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9maW5hbmNlL3dhb=

-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the =
Wall Street Journal</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The concept is being bandied about on Wall Str=
eet as investors and bankers search for ways to keep the money flowing. The=
 contradictory moniker refers to stock sales in which early backers private=
ly sell to longer-term investors such as mutual funds or sovereign-wealth f=
unds, sidestepping the traditional IPO process.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Private IPOs don=E2=80=99t come with the splas=
hy bell-ringing ceremony of a traditional debut or result in publicly trade=
d stock. They do allow companies to avoid the potential embarrassment of a =
new listing falling flat.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some on Wall Street balk at the name, seeing i=
t as window dressing for private placements, which are sales of shares from=
 one private owner to another and have been used for years. Skeptics say ba=
nkers, never a group to sit still, are playing with semantics to drum up bu=
siness.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What=E2=80=99s more, some mutual-fund managers=
 who have been approached to do private IPOs are wary. While they might get=
 better prices and bigger allotments of shares in a private IPO, the liquid=
ity=E2=80=94or lack of it=E2=80=94is a big drawback. If the managers buy st=
akes this way, it isn=E2=80=99t clear when they would be able to sell them.=
</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/34478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=

24a319a23cBb1aa71fc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">  talked about this phenomenon before</a>, an=
d I have argued that it kind of doesn=E2=80=99t make sense? In general, you=
 would expect the public markets to put a higher valuation on reasonably ma=
ture companies than the private markets, because liquidity is valuable, and=
 public markets are much more liquid. You should pay more for stock that yo=
u can sell than for stock that you can=E2=80=99t sell. If you are a company=

think you=E2=80=99ll get a good enough price, why would you get a <em>bette=

>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Maybe private investors are systemati=

tom: 5px;">Maybe <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Last week I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=

lid; background-color: #ccc;">  described BOXX</a>, the Alpha Architect 1-3=
 Month Box ETF, an exchange-traded fund that is meant to pay money-market i=
nterest rates without taxes, using options strategies. I wrote: =E2=80=9CTh=
e first thing you want to do is turn interest income into capital gains. =
=E2=80=A6 In some sense this should be <em>easy</em>.=E2=80=9D Some assets =
pay you a return that is called =E2=80=9Cinterest,=E2=80=9D which is fixed =
and paid in installments and taxed at ordinary income rates; other assets p=
ay you a return that is called =E2=80=9Cappreciation,=E2=80=9D which you re=
ceive when you sell the asset and which is taxed at lower capital gains rat=
es. There is some economic equivalence between these things; assets that do=
n=E2=80=99t pay interest generally appreciate at some rate that compensates=
 you for the use=C2=A0of your money, sort of like interest does.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The trick is to find an asset that appreciates=
 at the risk-free rate, i.e., that reliably pays you roughly the same retur=
n as short-term Treasury bills, but in the form of =E2=80=9Cappreciation=E2=
=80=9D rather than =E2=80=9Cinterest.=E2=80=9D The simplest way to do that =
would be with a zero-coupon bond =E2=80=94=C2=A0a bond that doesn=E2=80=99t=
 make quarterly interest payments, but just pays you back more at the end t=
han you put in at the beginning =E2=80=94=C2=A0but that=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>=
too=C2=A0</em>simple, and the US tax code looks through that trick and trea=
ts the appreciation on a zero-coupon bond as interest.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So the second-simplest way to do it=C2=A0is by=
 buying a=C2=A0<em>stock=C2=A0</em>(a classic capital asset) and=C2=A0<em>h=
edging out the stock-price risk</em>. For instance, if you buy a stock and =
simultaneously sell it in a fixed-price forward contract, you have effectiv=
ely hedged out the stock-price risk. And that=E2=80=99s roughly what BOXX d=
oes, in the form of =E2=80=9Cbox spreads=E2=80=9D on S&P 500 index opti=
ons (get synthetically long the index by buying a call option and selling a=
 put, get synthetically short the index by selling a call and buying a put,=
 and do those things at different strike prices so that you lock in some pa=
yoff in the future).=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But several readers emailed to point out that,=
 at least sometimes,=C2=A0the US tax code looks through=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0=
</em>trick too. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34=
478802.258380/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L3VzY29kZS90ZXh0LzI2LzEyN=

!important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-=
bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Section 1258</a> of the Inte=
rnal Revenue Code recharacterizes some capital gains as ordinary income, if=
 those gains are=C2=A0<em>effectively=C2=A0</em>interest on a =E2=80=9Cconv=
ersion transaction,=E2=80=9D meaning a transaction where you (1) buy an ass=
et and (2) simultaneously enter into a contract to sell the asset at a fixe=
d price.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-colo=
r: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a>=C2=A0So a simple transaction like =E2=80=9Cbuy a stock and sell it for=
ward=E2=80=9D probably produces ordinary income, not capital gains.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Do BOXX=E2=80=99s box spreads produce ordinary=
 income? My understanding is that there is a=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.m=

=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">common view</a>=C2=A0that box spreads are=C2=A0<em>not </em>=
=E2=80=9Cconversion transactions,=E2=80=9D and in particular that box sprea=
ds with=C2=A0<em>index</em> options are covered by different rules. But I d=
o not claim to be an expert, or to give tax advice, and apparently some peo=
ple=C2=A0disagree.=C2=A0</p>
